entry.title: Re: Can SAP Joule Skills Be Triggered by External Events (e.g., Incoming Email)?
entry.content:
Thanks for your response. I agree that the external event itself is just the trigger.

My intention isn't to use Joule merely as an event-processing engine. The idea is that an external event (such as an email arriving in a shared mailbox) initiates a workflow, and from that point the Joule Agent performs agentic AI tasks—such as understanding the email context, reasoning over enterprise data, deciding the next course of action, orchestrating business processes, interacting with SAP and non-SAP systems, and taking appropriate actions autonomously where applicable.

So my question is specifically about whether Joule Agents/Skills can be invoked programmatically by an external event, instead of only through a user interaction in the Joule interface. I'm interested in understanding if SAP currently supports this event-driven invocation pattern or if there are recommended architectures to achieve it.

entry.title: Default User Layout in Material Documents Overview App
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We would like to set a user-specific default layout in the Material Documents Overview app so that the same layout is automatically applied whenever the app is accessed, either directly or indirectly.

We tested this behavior in the SAP Public Cloud environment. The default layout works correctly when the app is accessed directly. However, when the Material Documents Overview app is launched from another application, such as Display Stock Overview (MMBE), the system resets the layout to the standard default instead of retaining the user-specific layout.

As a result, users must manually select and apply their preferred layout each time, which impacts reporting consistency and requires additional time and effort.

Please confirm whether this is the standard system behavior or if there is a configuration or enhancement available to ensure that the user-specific default layout is retained regardless of how the app is accessed?

entry.title: Re: Can SAP Joule Skills Be Triggered by External Events (e.g., Incoming Email)?
entry.content:
Thanks for the suggestion. I agree that SAP Business Process Automation is a good fit for handling the event trigger and workflow orchestration.

My question is more about what happens after that. For example, BPA detects the incoming email and starts a process, but instead of implementing all the business logic in the workflow, can it invoke a Joule Agent to perform agentic AI tasks such as analyzing the email, reasoning over enterprise context, deciding the next best action, and then returning the outcome to the process?

I'm trying to understand whether this pattern—BPA for event orchestration + Joule Agent for AI-driven reasoning and execution—is supported today or if SAP recommends a different architecture.

entry.title: Re: Writing (Posting) Data to Non-Leaf Members in Advance...
entry.content:
@79 

By default Its not possible to write as a target to non leaf members of a dimension. Since parents are supposed to show the aggregation of members below it.

To be able to write to a non leaf member, you would need to create a alternate hierarchy where these non leaf nodes are leaf. In your Data action you need to point to this alternate hierarchy and it will allow you to post values on that.

The concept is explained in the below section of help document.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/1679129715024574b7bbb4a611471765.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=release

 

Regarding your design, you could first aggregate the data by posting on parent members using alternate hierarchy. Maintain attributes for cost breakdown derivation on these parents and then finally derive the cost breakdown on this aggregated data. Once derivation is complete and reconciled, you could delete the intermediate data at parents without cost breakdown ( ="#")

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Query on Creating a Generic OData Connection in SAP A...
entry.content:
@arjun_tko 

Its not possible to work with a generic Odata connection in SAC. Only a single OData service per connection can be consumed and you must provide the connection with a full OData service path per OData connection.

Its covered in the below Note Point 8, Note 2.

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2628688

2628688 - OData service connection in SAP Analytics Cloud ***Collective KBA***

 

Hope this helps!

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Query on Creating a Generic OData Connection in SAP A...
entry.content:
Hello @arjun_tko 

A single generic SAC OData connection cannot be used for multiple OData services. Separate connections must be created for each OData service URL.

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Writing (Posting) Data to Non-Leaf Members in Advance...
entry.content:
Hello @79 

No, Advanced Formulas cannot write data directly to non-leaf (parent) members. Data can only be written to leaf/base members.

Create a separate mapping dimension/model containing:

G/L Account (or Account Group)
Organization (or Organization Group)
Business
Cost Breakdown Category
Use RESULTLOOKUP() and LINK() in Advanced Formulas to assign the category.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Forms Label Translation
entry.content:
Hello @Reserve 

There is no standard translation application, CDS view, or API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that automatically translates arbitrary form labels (e.g., English → Dutch) based on input text.
Maintain multilingual texts directly in SAP Forms Service by Adobe templates.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite: A new paradigm for API & Integration practitioner
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

Introduction

Working with SAP Integration Suite, you know the depth it brings across its two most valued capabilities: Cloud Integration (CPI), the engine behind message mediation, transformation, and process orchestration, and API Management, where APIs are governed, secured, protected, and published. Together, they power and integrate some of the most complex enterprise landscapes in the world. And yet, as mature and capable as each is in its own right, there's a natural question that practitioners regularly ask: what if these two capabilities truly converged? What if the design experience, the runtime, and the observability layer spoke a single language, one that felt native to both the integration developer and the API architect? That question is what API-Centric Integration is designed to answer.

One Coin. Two Sides. One Experience.

Practitioners have long known that API management and integration are not independent concerns. Every API is an integration surface. Every integration exposes a contract that could benefit from API-level governance. The separation has been more a consequence of how solutions and their technical stacks evolved than a reflection of how the work actually flows.

API-Centric Integration is built on a simple but powerful premise: the design language, the runtime, and the observability plane should be unified, empowered by AI. Not loosely coupled. Not bridged after the fact. Unified by design.

What This Means in Practice

A common design language

Customers can now define, secure, and manage APIs using a single, consistent authoring experience that natively supports mediation and augmentation steps. The modeling itself occurs on a newly introduced artifact type simply called the API, and this, too, has been consciously kept distinct. It is not the same as an IFlow in Cloud Integration, and it is not the same as the API proxy or API artifact you may know from the classic API Management functionality. It is a first-principles rethink of what an API artifact should look like when integration capability is native to it, not layered on top.

If you are a Cloud Integration practitioner, the concepts inside it will feel familiar: routing, transformation, enrichment, logging. If you are an API architect, the governance constructs like policies, rate limiting, and security schemes are first-class citizens. The two disciplines that have long lived on separate stacks, with separate tools and separate mental models, now meet inside a single artifact.

Underneath this experience runs a single, SAP-managed cloud-native runtime called the Integration Cell. It exists to make the unified design experience above possible, with the deployment, scaling, and execution details handled for you.

 

Agent-readiness, with MCP Gateway

This is perhaps the most forward-looking aspect of API-Centric Integration. APIs built within this new model can be elevated to MCP Servers, the protocol standard for exposing tools and data to AI agents and large language models, without additional development effort. It is a deliberate architectural choice to ensure that the APIs you build today are natively consumable by the agentic applications and Joule-powered workflows of tomorrow. The API you publish for a human consumer and the tools you expose to an AI agent are, again, two sides of the same coin. 

Follow this post to learn more about the MCP Gateway.

 

A single observability pane

One of the most persistent operational challenges in running both stacks has been complexity: separate monitoring dashboards, separate log pipelines, and no single place to understand what is actually happening across your API and integration landscape.

API-Centric Integration changes that. It runs on a single execution engine with a unified pipeline for logs and traces. When something needs attention, you look in one place.

But observability in API-Centric Integration goes further than just consolidating logs. The same unified pane brings in analytics capabilities that were previously either absent or fragmented. Usage patterns, traffic trends, and performance metrics across your APIs and MCP servers are visible together, in a single dashboard, broken down by the dimensions that actually matter: by API, by consumer, by agent, by tool.

For teams that need to stay ahead of problems rather than just react to them, the analytics layer includes AI-based anomaly detection that learns the normal traffic behaviour of your APIs and flags deviations before they become incidents. Alongside that, traffic predictions give you a forward-looking view of expected API call volumes, so capacity planning and resource decisions are grounded in data rather than guesswork.

The result is an observability experience that is not just unified, but genuinely intelligent. One place to look, whether you are investigating a live issue, reviewing consumption trends, or planning for what comes next.

A simplified commercial model

For integrations built with this new design, there is no double-counting across the API and integration layers. You are running one thing. You pay for one thing.

A foundation for AI Gateway capabilities

Beyond MCP, a rich AI Gateway will be integrated into this design experience over time, bringing model routing, token governance, and LLM observability into the same unified surface. The integration layer is becoming the AI governance layer. API-Centric Integration is where that convergence begins.

What this is, and what it isn't

API-Centric Integration is relevant for greenfield projects: new APIs, new integration flows, new architectures built on a clean slate. Existing APIs managed through the current API Management capability and existing CPI iFlows continue to operate exactly as they are.

Think of it as the recommended starting point for net-new work, the architecture you would choose if you were starting today, knowing what you now know about where integration and AI are heading.

One additional note on scope: the Integration Cell is a new runtime introduced specifically for this paradigm. It sits alongside, not on top of, your existing Cloud Integration runtime and Edge Integration Cell. These are not competing runtimes. They are the right tool for the right job, depending on where you are in your journey.

The bigger picture

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. What is changing is the nature of what needs to be connected and what needs to consume those connections. Business processes increasingly involve AI agents that require governed, observable, low-latency access to enterprise data and functions. In that world, the distinction between an integration runtime and an API runtime becomes not just unnecessary, but actively counterproductive.

API-Centric Integration is our answer to that shift: a unified foundation that respects the depth of what practitioners have built, while opening the door to a design paradigm that is ready for the agent-first era.

Getting started

API-Centric Integration is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite. Full product documentation, including a guided walkthrough of the API Artifact, policy modelling, and Integration Cell setup, is available on the SAP Help Portal:

API-Centric Integration - SAP Help Portal 

Rollout status

The rollout is actively in progress across SAP's managed landscape:

  • Trial landscape: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 1 data centers: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 2 data centers: Currently receiving software updates and feature enablement.

For the full rollout schedule and data center details, refer to SAP Note 3418154 .

If you are an existing SAP Integration Suite customer, no action is required on your part to receive the update. When the rollout reaches your data center, the capability will be available in your tenant. Your existing integration flows and API proxies are unaffected.

We will be sharing more on hands-on guidance, and product deep-dives in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: Custom Field Not Visible in FAGLL03H Report Output or Layout in PRD
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have extended transaction FAGLL03H by implementing the FAGL_LIB  BAdI (IF_FAGL_LIB~SELECT_DATA) to display a custom field in the report output.

Implementation Details

  • BAdI: FAGL_LIB
  • Method Implemented: IF_FAGL_LIB~SELECT_DATA
  • Custom field appended to:
    • SGLPOS_N_CT
    • SGLPOS_N_GL_CT

Issue

The enhancement is working as expected in both Development (DEV) and Quality (QAS) systems. The custom field is available in the report output and can also be added through the layout.

However, in the Production (PRD) system, the custom field is not available in the report output or in the layout field selection.

Analysis Performed

  • The append structures are present and active in SE11 in PRD.
  • The BAdI implementation is active in PRD.
  • The transport has been imported successfully.
  • During debugging in IF_FAGL_LIB~SELECT_DATA,  I observed that the custom field is not available in <ls_data> (LIKE LINE OF CT_DATA) or in CT_DATA. 
  • One difference I noticed is that transaction HDBVIES was not executed in PRD after transporting the enhancement, although it is mentioned as a post-transport step in the SAP documentation/blog for extending FAGLL03H.

Queries

  1. Could the absence of the custom field in CT_DATA be due to HDBVIEWS not being executed in PRD?
  2. Is executing HDBVIEWS mandatory after appending custom fields to SGLPOS_N_CT and SGLPOS_N_GL_CT?
  3. Is it sufficient to regenerate only the relevant HDB views, or is it necessary to regenerate all HDB views?
  4. If all HDB views are regenerated in the Production system, is there any risk of impacting existing custom developments or standard reports?
  5. Are there any additional activation or post-transport steps that should be performed to make the custom field available in FAGLL03H?

I would appreciate any suggestions or guidance from anyone who has encountered a similar issue.

Thank you.

entry.title: Built an MCP Server for SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

I'm Jayesh Sinha, an SAP Integration Suite Architect with over 11 years of experience designing and implementing integration solutions across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. Throughout my career, I have worked extensively with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), helping organizations build scalable, reliable, and maintainable integration flows. This hands-on experience with CPI's monitoring, troubleshooting, and operational challenges is what inspired me to explore how AI agents and the Model Context Protocol can simplify day-to-day integration work. This blog reflects my journey of combining enterprise integration expertise with modern AI tooling to make CPI operations more conversational and efficient.

SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CI, is widely used for designing, deploying, and monitoring integration flows between SAP and non-SAP systems. While CPI provides strong monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities, checking message logs, failed messages, runtime artifacts, and integration packages usually requires manual navigation inside the SAP Integration Suite UI.

In this blog, we will build a Python-based MCP server that connects to SAP CI and exposes CI operations as tools. Then, we will connect an AI agent to this MCP server so the agent can inspect CI logs, identify failed messages, summarize issues, and help with troubleshooting.

Instead of manually opening CPI, filtering logs, and reading technical payloads, the AI agent can call predefined CPI tools through the MCP server and return a clear summary.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way for AI applications and agents to connect with external tools, systems, APIs, files, and data sources.

In simple terms, MCP allows an AI agent to use tools safely and consistently.

For example, instead of giving an AI model direct access to SAP CPI credentials and asking it to figure out API calls, we create an MCP server that exposes controlled tools

High-Level Architecture

User Queation -> AI Agent-> MCP Client -> Python MCP Server -> SAP CPI OData APIs -> CPI Logs, Artifacts, Packages, Metadata

The MCP server is responsible for:

Authenticating with SAP CPI
Calling CPI OData APIs
Handling JSON responses
Exposing CPI actions as MCP tools
Keeping credentials outside the AI prompt
Providing a controlled access layer

Benefits of This Approach

This solution provides several benefits.

It makes CPI troubleshooting more conversational. Developers can ask questions instead of manually navigating logs.

It separates AI reasoning from enterprise system access. The AI agent does not directly handle CPI credentials.

It creates a reusable integration layer. The same MCP server can be used by different MCP-compatible clients.

It improves maintainability. CPI API logic is centralized in one server.

It is extensible. More CPI tools can be added later as needed.

It supports safer automation. Read-only tools can be introduced first, while write operations can be added later with stronger controls.

Configuration

you can use CHATGPT/CLAUDE Desktop to connect to the MCP server.

1) Download MCP server in you local system from below GIT repository.

https://github.com/jacksnh/CPI-MCP-SERVER 

2) Modify "claude_desktop_config.json" to add the MCP server

"ci-mcp-server2": {
    "command": "python",
    "args": [
        "C:\\Users\\Username\\path\\to\\your\\server.py"
    ]
}

3) Restart Claude desktop and you will see below Server in Developer Section

4) Now your AI agent is ready to interact with MCP server

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Cloud Integration with an AI agent through an MCP server represents a meaningful step forward in how developers and integration teams interact with enterprise middleware. Rather than manually sifting through logs and navigating the SAP Integration Suite UI, teams can now ask plain-language questions and receive clear, actionable answers in seconds.

entry.title: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan  (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

This post is a follow-up to our earlier introduction of API-Centric Integration. If you haven't read that yet, we recommend starting there.

Introduction

The way enterprise software gets used is changing. AI agents, autonomous programs that reason, plan, and take action, are no longer a future concept. They are arriving in production environments now, and they need to access your enterprise systems, data, and processes to be useful.

The question for every enterprise architect and IT leader is the same: how do you let AI agents into your systems without losing the governance and control you have spent years building?

In our previous post, we introduced API-Centric Integration: a unified design and runtime experience that brings API governance and integration mediation together in a single artifact. We closed with a forward-looking note on agent-readiness. This post is where that story continues.

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the answer.

A quick glossary before we begin

MCP is still a relatively new term in most enterprise conversations, so it is worth grounding the three concepts that matter here before going further.

An MCP Tool is a named, callable entity that an AI agent can discover and invoke. Think of it as a function with a description: the agent reads that description, decides whether the tool is relevant to the task at hand, and calls it. A tool might represent "Get Customer Order Status," "Reconcile Inventory Levels Across Distribution Centers," or "Trigger a Purchase Order Approval." Tools are the atomic unit of agent-accessible functionality.

An MCP Server is the runtime host that exposes one or more tools over the Model Context Protocol, an open standard that any MCP-compatible agent or framework can connect to. If tools are the endpoints, the MCP Server is the API server hosting them. It makes tools discoverable and invokable in a standardized way, decoupled from how the underlying system actually works.

An MCP Gateway is the enterprise control plane for MCP. Where an MCP Server hosts tools, the Gateway governs access to them: enforcing authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and payload protection, and providing full monitoring and traceability. SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway aggregates tools from SAP APIs, non-SAP APIs, integration flows, data sources, and external MCP Servers into a single governed entry point.

The problem with the way things work today - and why your existing investment is the solution

Most enterprises that have invested in SAP Integration Suite already have a curated, governed library of APIs: connecting SAP S/4HANA, Business AI Platform, third-party systems, and custom backends. That represents years of work: designing integrations, securing them, documenting them, and operationalizing them.

The challenge until now has been that none of that investment was directly accessible to AI agents. Agents do not call REST APIs natively the way developers do. They need tools: described, discoverable, and callable via a standardized protocol. Without that, every agent project forces the same painful workaround: a developer manually reads the backend schema and translates it into the agent's tool-calling code, field names, types, nested structures, all hardcoded. That mapping is brittle. If the backend schema changes, nothing tells the agent. The integration breaks, silently. The next project repeats the same work from zero. And security and governance are only as consistent as each developer's diligence.

The agent can reason. It still cannot reach your systems without someone hand-building the bridge, every time.

MCP Gateway closes that gap without asking you to rebuild anything. Your curated APIs become agent-ready tools. Your existing governance models: RBAC, rate limits, and monitoring carry forward into the agentic layer. The work you have done is not a legacy to migrate away from. It is the platform your AI strategy runs on.

Introducing MCP Gateway

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is organized around three things:

Connect. Turn APIs and services into discoverable, reusable tools for agent use. API specifications are automatically translated into MCP schema and augmented with natural language semantics, so agents understand not just the structure of a tool but what it is for. No manual schema translation. No hardcoding. Backend connectivity follows familiar conventions: API Providers and BTP Destinations are used to discover and connect to SAP and non-SAP backend systems, so the connectivity model your teams already rely on carries forward into the agentic layer. Developer Hub extends naturally from an API catalog to a tool and MCP catalog: developers and agent builders can discover available MCP tools, understand their purpose and schema, and subscribe, using the same experience they already use for APIs

Govern. Apply policies, authentication, and usage controls for agents to access tools and systems. Rate limiting, IP blocking, authentication based on OIDC from SAP  (XSUAA) and third-party identity providers, and payload protection all apply to agent traffic with the same rigor as human-facing API traffic. Agents only see and can invoke the tools they are explicitly authorised for: essential in multi-agent environments where different agents have different roles and should have different access boundaries.

Analyze. Provide visibility and traceability across agents, tools, and enterprise landscapes. Every tool invocation by every agent is logged with full traceability, supporting compliance requirements, operational governance, and usage analytics. You have the same visibility into agent behavior that you have today into the transactions handled by SAP Integration Suite, including comprehensive logs, traces, and intelligent analytics.

 

MCP Gateway as the endorsed pathway for SAP API access

This is not a standalone capability sitting outside SAP's broader architecture thinking. SAP's Architecture Center reference architecture for A2A and MCP for Interoperability defines how enterprise AI agent ecosystems should be structured, and SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway sits at its core.

In practical terms, if you are deploying AI agents that interact with SAP systems, MCP Gateway is not just a convenient option. It is SAP's architecturally and policy-endorsed approach to doing so in a compliant, governed, and auditable way.

The recommendation is clear: external third-party agents should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Customer-managed pro-code and low-code agents deployed in the SAP BTP context should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Third-party MCP Servers should federate with the MCP Gateway to access SAP systems.

An important distinction: MCP is a connectivity protocol, not a semantic layer

The Architecture Center makes a point here that is easy to overlook and consequential if you do.

MCP standardizes how agents call tools and APIs. It does not add business context or improve the accuracy of AI results. Connecting an MCP server directly to raw SAP transactional APIs without semantic enrichment leads to poor entity discovery accuracy, excessive token consumption, and, for write operations, a real risk of incorrect business transactions. An agent that calls a raw OData endpoint without understanding what the fields mean, or without the business context to distinguish a purchase order from a blanket agreement, will reason poorly and act incorrectly.

This is why the design of an MCP tool matters as much as the platform that hosts it. MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite handles the governed, enterprise-grade concerns: authentication, authorization, rate limiting, payload protection, and observability across SAP and non-SAP APIs. What it does not do, and is not designed to do -> is to decide how to shape your tools.

An API Artifact that exposes nine atomic CRUD endpoints as nine separate tools will produce an MCP Server that forces an agent to invent the orchestration logic a good tool designer should have encoded. The right instinct is to design tools around business outcomes rather than API operations: find_at_risk_suppliers_for_material rather than GET /assmnt/PlantMaterialRisk/{plantId}. The platform enables either. The design is yours.

Two typical use cases

The following scenarios show what becomes possible when your existing SAP investment meets the agentic layer.

Use Case 1: Third-Party AI Platform Accessing SAP Business Data

A retailer uses Microsoft Copilot Studio to build procurement agents for their buying team. The agents need to query SAP product catalogs, check supplier agreements, and initiate purchase requisitions. Historically, this required a custom integration project where developers hand-translated backend schemas into tool-calling code.

With the MCP Gateway, SAP's procurement APIs are exposed as MCP tools through a hosted MCP Server. Copilot Studio agents connect via the standard MCP protocol, authenticate via OIDC, and invoke only the tools they are authorized for. The IT team governs access from a single control plane in Integration Suite, fully aligned with the endorsed architecture from SAP's Architecture Center.

Use Case 2: Multi-Agent HR Process Automation on SAP BTP

An enterprise deploys multiple specialized agents on SAP BTP for HR: one for onboarding, one for payroll queries, one for performance data. Each is built differently: the onboarding agent is low-code via Joule Studio (fka SAP Build), the payroll agent is pro-code using SAP Cloud SDK for AI.

Both agents access SAP SuccessFactors and third-party HR data sources via the MCP Gateway. Tool authorization is scoped per agent role: the onboarding agent cannot access payroll tools, and vice versa. The architecture follows SAP's golden path for agentic AI interoperability.

What it shields you from

MCP is still evolving rapidly. The specification has already undergone significant architectural changes in 2026. Running self-managed MCP servers means tracking that specification roadmap closely, managing upgrade cycles, and absorbing the security implications of each revision. Both patterns of self-managed MCP servers, whether hosted externally or on SAP BTP, place the full operational responsibility for platform stability, security hardening, credential lifecycle, and spec compatibility squarely on the customer or partner deploying them.

For a detailed breakdown of the cross-cutting concerns every MCP operator must address, including authentication, token exchange, rate limiting, observability, and lifecycle management, the Third-Party MCP Access reference architecture on SAP's Architecture Center is the definitive starting point.

The bigger picture

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the natural evolution of the API Gateway into the agentic era. The core promise is the same: bring your systems together under governed, observable, secure access. The difference is that the consumer is no longer just a developer or an application. It is an AI agent.

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. MCP Gateway is what makes that connective tissue agent-aware. The APIs you have already built, the integration flows already running, the S/4HANA function modules already deployed: all of it can now be made consumable by agents through a single governed surface, with the same authentication, observability, and policy enforcement you apply to human-facing traffic today.

Getting started

MCP Gateway is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite Premium and Enhanced Editions. Full documentation is available on the SAP Help Portal. You can also get started by trying it out using the SAP BTP trial or the Free Service Plan.

We will be sharing deep dives and hands-on tutorials in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: How to Identify Key Fields from a Released CDS View in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and need to retrieve metadata for a released CDS view, specifically the fields that are defined as key fields.

Currently, I am using the following ABAP code to retrieve the field/component information:

DATA(lo_struct) = CAST cl_abap_structdescr(
                    cl_abap_typedescr=>describe_by_name( p_tabname )
                  ).

 

DATA(lt_dfies_c) = lo_struct->get_components( ).

This successfully returns the components/fields of the CDS view structure. However, I do not see any information indicating whether a field is defined as a KEY in the CDS view.

My requirement is to programmatically determine which fields are key fields for a released CDS view in the ABAP Cloud/Public Cloud environment.

Could anyone advise:

  • Is there a supported API or class in ABAP Cloud to retrieve CDS metadata including key-field information?
  •  
  • Are there any restrictions in SAP Public Cloud that prevent access to CDS key definitions at runtime?

    Any guidance or recommended approach would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Welcome Everyone to the APAC Digital Hub
entry.content:
I am delighted to see this space launch as part of SAP ACC APAC 2026.

As educators, we often leave a conference full of ideas, new contacts, and conversations that we wish could continue once we return home. This Digital Hub gives us that opportunity.

While the conference may only last a few days, our community operates year-round. Whether you are looking for advice on teaching with SAP, exploring the impact of AI in education, sharing classroom experiences, discussing curriculum design, or connecting with colleagues across the region, I encourage you to use this space as an ongoing conversation rather than something that ends when the conference does.

As President of the SAP University Alliances Asia Pacific Academic Board, one of the things I value most about this community is the willingness of members to share openly with one another. Some of the best ideas presented at past conferences started as informal discussions between educators facing similar challenges in their classrooms.

Whether you attended a Train-the-Trainer session, Mini Workshop, Teaching Seminar, Research Seminar, New Faculty Seminar, or conference presentation, I encourage you to share.

As an example, I've recently shared a blog post, "Teaching SAP Without Teaching SAP", where I reflect on how I use enterprise technologies to support experiential learning and help students connect theory with practice.

It is just one example of the types of conversations, ideas, and experiences I hope we will continue to share through this Digital Hub.

I encourage you to contribute your own:

  • One idea you'll take back and try
  • A question that is still on your mind
  • An approach that has worked in your classroom
  • A challenge where our community might be able to help

I'll be actively following discussions here and look forward to continuing many of the conversations that begin during the conference.  And don't forget to use the "SAP ACC APAC Digital Hub" tag when posting content related to our region or conference. It will make it much easier for all of us to find, follow, and learn from the growing collection of APAC discussions and ideas.

Thank you to the SAP UA (Academic Partnership)  team for creating this Digital Hub. Let's work together to make it a vibrant space for collaboration, innovation, and shared learning across our community.

Looking forward to connecting with you all.

Matt Dyki
President, SAP University Alliances Asia Pacific Academic Board
Teaching Fellow, The University of Melbourne

entry.title: How to Add a Custom Formatter in SAPUI5: View vs. Controller Approach
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Introduction

When building SAPUI5 applications, we often bind data directly from a model to the UI. While simple property binding works well for displaying raw values, there are many scenarios where the displayed value needs additional formatting before it reaches the user. For example, you might want to combine multiple fields into a single sentence, display semantic colors based on a value, or convert technical data into a more user-friendly format.

This is where custom formatters become useful. They allow you to keep formatting logic separate from your XML views, making your application easier to read, maintain, and reuse.

In this blog, we'll explore:

  • What a custom formatter is and why you should use one.
  • Two different ways to integrate an external formatter into your SAPUI5 application:
    • Using core:require directly in the XML View.
    • Importing the formatter through the Controller.
  • A practical example that demonstrates both approaches side by side using real code and application output.
  • When to use each approach, so you can choose the one that best fits your application's structure and development style.

Project Context

While practicing SAPUI5, I wanted to organize my formatting logic outside the XML View instead of writing formatting code directly in bindings. During this exercise, I discovered that SAPUI5 provides more than one way to use external formatter files.

I experimented with both approaches, loading the formatter directly in the XML View using core:require and importing it through the controller. Since both approaches produce the same result but differ in how the formatter is referenced, I thought it would be useful to compare them side by side with a simple practical example.

What is a Custom Formatter, and Why Use One

A custom formatter is a JavaScript function that transforms model data before it is displayed in the UI. It allows you to present data in a more meaningful or user-friendly format without modifying the original data in the model. Since a formatter only affects the displayed value, it supports one-way binding and cannot be used for two-way (editable) bindings.

Some common use cases include:

  • Converting technical values into user-friendly text.
  • Combining multiple model properties into a single display value.
  • Applying semantic states such as Success, Warning, or Error based on a value.
  • Formatting dates, currency, or performing simple calculations before displaying data.

In the following sections, we'll implement these concepts with a practical SAPUI5 example using reusable external formatter files.

Project Setup and Folder Structure

Here's the folder structure used for this example, a simple table of users with their age and salary, where we'll apply one formatter for each column:

project1/                        ← SAPUI5 application
└── webapp/
    ├── controller/               ← Controller logic
    │   ├── App.controller.js
    │   └── View1.controller.js   ← attaches AgeFormatter (controller approach)
    ├── formatter/                ← Reusable external formatter files
    │   ├── AgeFormatter.js       ← used via controller
    │   └── SalaryFormatter.js    ← used via core:require in the view
    ├── model/
    │   ├── models.js
    │   └── users.json            ← sample data
    ├── view/
    │   ├── App.view.xml
    │   └── View1.view.xml        ← table using both formatter approaches
    ├── Component.js
    ├── index.html
    └── manifest.json             ← model & routing configuration

For this example, I created two separate formatter files to demonstrate both approaches independently. One formatter is loaded directly in the XML View using core:require, while the other is imported through the controller. This makes it easier to understand how each approach works without mixing the implementations.

  • AgeFormatter.js – Combines a user's name and age into a readable sentence.
  • SalaryFormatter.js – Maps a salary value to an appropriate semantic state.

Sample Data (users.json)

The application uses a simple JSON model containing user information. The age and salary properties are used by the formatter functions in the following examples.

{
  "users": [
    { "id": 1, "name": "Monkey D. Luffy", "age": 16, "salary": 22000 },
    { "id": 2, "name": "Roronoa Zoro", "age": 21, "salary": 64000 },
    { "id": 3, "name": "Nami", "age": 20, "salary": 2400 },
    { "id": 4, "name": "Usopp", "age": 19, "salary": 2500 },
    { "id": 5, "name": "Sanji", "age": 21, "salary": 7000 },
    { "id": 6, "name": "Tony Tony Chopper", "age": 17, "salary": 6800 },
    { "id": 7, "name": "Nico Robin", "age": 30, "salary": 9200 },
    { "id": 8, "name": "Franky", "age": 36, "salary": 88000 },
    { "id": 9, "name": "Brook", "age": 90, "salary": 33000 },
    { "id": 10, "name": "Jinbe", "age": 46, "salary": 440000 }
  ]
}

Registering the Model (manifest.json)

"oModel": {
    "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel",
    "uri": "model/users.json"
}

The JSON model is registered in manifest.json, making it available throughout the application using the model name oModel.

Approach 1: Using core:require in the View

This approach loads the formatter module directly in the XML view using the core:require attribute, and gives it an alias you can reference in any binding inside that view. No changes are needed in the controller.

SalaryFormatter.js

sap.ui.define([], function(){
    "use strict";
    return {
         /*
         * This formatter is required directly in the VIEW via core:require
         * (see View1.view.xml) and referenced as 'Salary.salaryState'.
         */
        salaryState: function(salary){
           if (salary >= 10000) {
                return "Success"; // green color
            } else if (salary >= 5000) {
                return "Warning"; // orange color
            } else {
                return "Error";   // red color
            }
        }
    }
})

While implementing this example, I kept the salary-related formatting in a dedicated formatter file instead of writing conditional logic directly in the XML View. This keeps the binding expression clean and makes the formatting logic reusable.

View1.view.xml

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    core:require="{ Salary: 'project1/formatter/SalaryFormatter' }">
    <!-- Approach 1 (view-based):
         core:require loads the SalaryFormatter module and makes it available
         under the alias "Salary" for use anywhere inside this view. -->
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <Table items="{oModel>/users}">
            <columns>
                <Column><Text text="ID" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Name" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Age" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Salary" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Age Classification" /></Column>
            </columns>
            <items>
                <ColumnListItem>
                    <Text text="{oModel>id}" />
                    <Text text="{oModel>name}" />
                    <Text text="{oModel>age}" />

                    <!-- "Salary" here refers to the alias declared in core:require above -->
                    <ObjectStatus text="{oModel>salary}" state="{path:'oModel>salary', formatter:'Salary.salaryState'}" />
                </ColumnListItem>
            </items>
        </Table>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

One thing I found convenient about this approach is that everything required by the formatter stays inside the XML View. Since the formatter is only used in this view, there was no need to modify the controller. This also keeps the formatter dependency close to where it is actually used.

Approach 2: Attaching the Formatter in the Controller

While exploring formatter implementations, I also tried importing the formatter through the controller. In this approach, the formatter is loaded as a controller dependency and then exposed to the XML View through the formatter property.

AgeFormatter.js

sap.ui.define([], function () {
    "use strict";

    return {
        // Classifies a person's age into a category and returns a readable sentence.
        ageClassification: function (sName, sAge) {
            var nAge = Number(sAge);
            var sCategory;

            if (nAge < 13) {
                sCategory = "Child";
            } else if (nAge < 18) {
                sCategory = "Youth";
            } else if (nAge < 60) {
                sCategory = "Adult";
            } else {
                sCategory = "Senior Citizen";
            }

            // Pick "a" or "an" based on whether the category starts with a vowel sound
            var sArticle = /^[aeiou]/i.test(sCategory) ? "an" : "a";

            return `${sName} is ${sArticle} ${sCategory}`;
        }
    };
});

View1.controller.js

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    // Import the formatter module here and attach it to the controller
    "project1/formatter/AgeFormatter"
], (Controller, AgeFormatter) => {
    "use strict";
    return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {

        // Attaching the module as "formatter" is what makes
        // ".formatter.ageClassification" resolvable from the view
        formatter: AgeFormatter,

        onInit() {
        }
    });
});
View1.view.xml (relevant part)
<!-- The leading dot means "look this up on the controller instance",
     i.e. this.formatter.ageClassification -->
<Text text="{parts:[{path:'oModel>name'}, {path:'oModel>age'}], formatter:'.formatter.ageClassification'}" />

Only the binding expression changes in the XML View. The remaining XML structure stays exactly the same as in the previous approach, so only the relevant binding is shown below.

Figure 1: XML View showing where each formatter approach is used

After implementing both approaches, I noticed that the application behavior and output remain exactly the same. The only difference is how the formatter module is made available to the XML View. This observation helped me understand that the choice is primarily about code organization rather than functionality.

Putting Both Together

Here's the same app with both formatters wired up, salary status via the view-based approach, age classification via the controller-based approach, running against a simple JSON model:

Figure 2: Output showing salary status and age classification for all users

Both approaches produce correct, working output. The difference is purely in where the dependency is declared and how it's referenced.

View vs. Controller Approach

Both approaches ultimately achieve the same goal. They allow you to keep formatting logic outside the XML View and produce the same output. After implementing both in the same application, I noticed that the primary difference isn't what they do, but where the formatter dependency is managed.

With the View approach (core:require), the formatter is imported directly into the XML View. This makes the view self-contained because anyone reading the XML can immediately see which formatter modules it depends on. I found this approach straightforward when the formatter is only used within a single view.

With the Controller approach, the formatter is imported as a controller dependency and then exposed to the XML View through the formatter property. This keeps all module imports together inside the controller, which can make the XML slightly cleaner. I found this approach useful because the controller becomes the single place responsible for managing its dependencies.

Another thing I observed is that the formatter implementation itself doesn't change. The same formatter function can be used with either approach. Only the way SAPUI5 resolves and accesses that function is different.

From a functionality perspective, both approaches produce identical results. The choice mainly comes down to how you prefer to organize your application and how your project structures reusable modules.

Conclusion

While building this example, I realized that both approaches ultimately achieve the same result. The difference lies in how the formatter dependency is organized rather than how the formatter behaves. Implementing and comparing both approaches side by side gave me a better understanding of how SAPUI5 resolves formatter functions, and I hope this practical comparison helps you make the right choice for your own applications.

Thank you for reading! If you've used a different approach or have additional insights, I'd love to hear about them in the comments.

entry.title: Re: Some questions for ERP LE and TM integration
entry.content:
Hello @EricYu0912 

Ad 1. The relevant configuration sits in SPRO (assuming you work with embedded TM): Transportation Management -> Integration -> Logistics Integration -> Internal TM Component Integration -> Define Transportation Relevance of Sales Documents and Define Transportation Relevance of Delivery Documents

Sales orders TM integration is defined by document type, sales area and shipping condition. Deliveries integration - by document type, shipping point and shipping condition.

Ad 2. FU type is determined from freight unit building rule (FUBR). FUBR is determined from logistics integration profile - either directly or with a condition. If FU type determined this way is actually a TU type, a TU is created instead of a FU. That is so called short cut scenario. FUBR can also determine FU type instead of FU type and create FOs directly.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: SAP CPI - Smart JMS Retry with Exception-Based Routing in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Hello @mitishah ,

Thank you for reading and sharing your thoughts!

To answer your first question: yes, I highly recommend this smart retry approach for production environments. When dealing with high message volumes, managing thread concurrency is crucial. This pattern ensures that worker nodes are not blocked by synchronous failures, keeping the tenant's overall performance stable.

Regarding common pitfalls and real-world lessons, the biggest trap is the overuse of the DLQ pattern. My strict approach is to implement JMS DLQs only if the business criticality of the integration is explicitly defined and truly justifies it.

Unfortunately, in the real world, this is rarely the case. Many companies and even integration architects fail to provide clear criticality classifications for their interfaces. What happens when this governance detail is missing? Developers defensively apply DLQs to all scenarios. This ultimately overloads the JMS broker, exhausts the tenant's retry mechanisms with non-critical messages, and creates a massive operational bottleneck.

So, my main design consideration is this: never implement a DLQ without a clear business requirement. Governance must drive the architecture, not the other way around.

Regards, Viana

entry.title: Component Colour Management with AVC and Material Variants in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) in SAP S/4HANA is a tool for managing complex configurable products. It's often positioned as the make-to-order or engineer-to-order sales configurator for S/4HANA, but it can also support different lightweight use cases. In this blog post, I will describe one manufacturing and logistics related use case: component colour management.

Use Case Introduction

Let’s introduce the use case with the classic bike example. Bikes are sold in many different colours, and the selected colour must be transferred from sales order item through BOM to relevant components. These components need the colour information so they can either be purchased in the correct colour or painted in-house. Not all components, like bike tires, are colour-relevant. Only colour-relevant components should be affected by the selected colour. The key challenge is logistics: different colours must be separated in inventory and in downstream logistics processes.

To put it as a business requirement:

  • Colour is only selected in the sales order item.
  • Only colour-relevant components inherit the colour.
  • Inventory must be separated by different colours.

Solution

The core idea is to model colour as an AVC characteristic on the finished product and use BOM class items to select the correct material variant for each colour-relevant component. Material variants are needed because colour must become logistics-relevant. It is possible to transfer the AVC colour characteristic to the generic component, but this has no impact on logistics processes. Alternatively, batch management could be used, but there is no standard automatic way to transfer AVC characteristics to batch classification.

The solution process flow is as follows:

  1. Colour is selected in the sales order item configuration.
  2. During BOM explosion, the BOM class item uses the colour value to determine the matching material variant.
  3. The selected coloured material variant is then used in downstream logistics processes such as procurement, production, inventory management, and picking.

Setup

The example below is based on S/4HANA Public Cloud, but the functionality should be the same in Private Cloud as well.

The first step is to define colour as a characteristic and assign it to an AVC class (type 300). In my example, the colour is defined with RAL codes, so the characteristic is a 4-digit numeric value.

Important note: to enable material variant selection through class items, the class must be material-specific. In my example, RM440 is the material number for the component. This means that a class must be created for each colour-relevant component. For a productive implementation, it probably makes sense to automate this.

Class and characteristic

Material variants must be created for all possible colour variants, with material-specific class assigned and colour value defined. Again, for a productive implementation, automation is recommended. Generic configurable “parent” component (here RM440) is not strictly mandatory, but makes managing the variants easier.

Material variantsExample of variant

To enable material variant selection based on the colour characteristic, a class item must be added to the BOM.

BOM with class item

The finished product must be configurable (KMAT) and have a class assigned with the colour characteristic. It’s not necessary to assign the material-specific class to the finished product, as long as the same colour characteristic is available there. In the example, I used a generic colour class, which also enables selection of multiple colour-relevant components based on one characteristic value in the sales order item.

Finished product with class assignment

Configuration profile must also exist for the finished product.

Finished product configuration profile

Demo

That’s the setup: no AVC dependencies are needed. Below is a short demo video showing how it works in simulation mode.

Conclusion

This setup provides a clean-core-friendly solution for component colour management where colour must be logistics-relevant. The main trade-off is material master complexity: using material variants leads to a high number of materials, making material master maintenance more challenging. For that reason, automation and proper master data management are important for productive use.

entry.title: Re: SAF-T Portugal - Incorrect Opening Balance calculatio...
entry.content:
Hi 6-Mar,

Short answer: The SAF-T opening balance is derived from the balance carryforward, so in-period adjustment journals can never realign it. The likely root cause is a non-standard mid-year load: the Jan–March balances were brought in on manually-created transfer accounts instead of the auto-reversing Historical Balance object, which doubled the opening. Fix the duplicate at its source, make the opening equal the true carryforward, then regenerate the file — and because this is a productive migrated ledger, treat it as a controlled correction, not a self-service journal.

Why the SAF-T opening ignores your adjustment postings

This is expected behavior, not a bug. SAF-T PT Accounting (report category PT_SAFT_ACCOUNTING) is generated in the Run Statutory Reports app, and the GeneralLedger section reports OpeningDebit/CreditBalance from the account's balance carryforward — not by re-summing your period documents — Reference: Generating the SAF-T Accounting File.

A journal posted in 2025 is a period movement (turnover): it affects the closing balance but never the reported opening. So the file keeps the carried-forward figure as opening, while your compensating entries sit in the movements, and Opening + Debit − Credit no longer ties to the book balance you corrected — Reference: Balance Carryforward.

Likely cause of the gap: the SAF-T value (56,863.33) matches the correct 31/12/2024 close, so it is most likely the correct opening; the doubled 113,726.66 is on the book side, from a duplicated migration/transfer posting that your adjustments then masked. (Deduction from your figures — 113,726.66 = 2 × 56,863.33 — confirm with the validation report below.)

Has anyone faced this after an S/4HANA migration?

Yes — and it usually comes from how the mid-year balances were loaded. For an in-year go-live, SAP's approach is one migration key date: balance-sheet accounts as the closing balance at the key date, P&L accounts as current-year movements from day one. Prior-year and prior-month detail (Jan–March) is meant to be loaded with the FI – Historical Balance object, which auto-reverses and is for reporting only — Reference: SAP KBA 2943035 (Migration of historical balances). When teams instead create their own transfer accounts and post those prior-period balances manually, the postings don't reverse, the opening is counted twice, and the carryforward that feeds SAF-T is distorted.

How mid-year migration should establish the opening (so you fix the right layer)

Operational balance: the FI – G/L account balance object posts only balances (no documents) to the migration key date, offset to an initial clearing account — Reference: FI – G/L account balance and open/line item.

Reporting history (Jan–March): the Historical Balance object loads it into already-closed periods and auto-creates the reversal, so it never disturbs the go-live balance or the carryforward.

Constraint: if the opening sits on hand-posted transfer accounts (not the Historical Balance object), those entries are real and non-reversing — that is where the duplicate lives.

Recommended correction approach (Public Cloud)

Fix the source, not the symptom.

  • Diagnose first. In Run Statutory Reports, before generating, run the General Ledger Account Validation Report — it lists each account with balance vs. expected balance and a semaphore, pinpointing 4310000001 and the exact gap that fails AT validation — Reference: Generating the SAF-T Accounting File.
  • Locate the duplicate. In Display G/L Account Balances, enter the account, company code and the cutover fiscal year, and see whether the extra 56,863.33 is in the period-0 carryforward, in the go-live migration document, or on the manually-created Jan–March transfer accounts.
  • Reverse the compensating adjustments (Manage Journal Entries) and remove the duplicated transfer posting at its origin. Net effect on the correct closing balance is zero.
  • If the opening itself must change: it only changes when you post into the previous fiscal year — the carryforward then updates automatically, or re-run it via Schedule General Ledger Jobs — Reference: Balance Carryforward. A current-year journal will not do it.
  • Regenerate and resubmit PT_SAFT_ACCOUNTING; if already submitted, produce the fix via a correction phase (a full corrected file is generated), not by editing the old run.

Important:

  • The productive load cannot be re-run or reset. The Reset Transaction Data job works only in the quality system; in production, corrections are manual journals against the migration offset account — Reference: Reset Transaction data 
  • Open an SAP incident (FI-LOC-SAF-PT / Document and Reporting Compliance) before correcting a productive migrated ledger to ensure the proper approach based on the real system data is taken

Summary

The SAF-T opening comes from the carryforward and can't be fixed with in-period journals. The most likely cause is that the Jan–March balances were hand-posted to created transfer accounts instead of the auto-reversing Historical Balance object, doubling the opening. Run the General Ledger Account Validation Report to confirm the layer, reverse the compensating adjustments and remove the duplicated posting at its origin (keeping the correct closing intact); only a prior-year posting plus a carryforward re-run actually moves the opening. Since the load can't be reset in production and prior periods may be closed, raise an SAP incident before touching production, then regenerate PT_SAFT_ACCOUNTING via a correction phase.

Hope it helps.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: SSL in HANA Cockpit
entry.content:
SSL Configuration in SAP HANA Cockpit.

SAP HANA Cockpit is a primary administration and recommended tool to manage SAP HANA databases. It provides some additional features and steps that are not available in HANA Studio.

 

Purpose.
This blog describes the steps to configure SSL for the SAP HANA Cockpit.

Overview: SSL Configuration for SAP HANA Cockpit.

Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) configuration for SAP HANA Cockpit is essential to ensure secure and encrypted communication between users (browsers) and the HANA Cockpit system.

 

Key Purpose.

Protect sensitive data from unauthorized access.
Ensure secure login and data transmission.


How It Works.

SSL uses digital certificates to encrypt communication.
The SAP HANA Cockpit is configured to use HTTPS instead of HTTP.
Certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) or created internally if you have SSO Server in your environment.

 

High-Level Steps.

HANA Cockpit Should be installed in your environment.
Technical Communication User should be created.
SYSTEM and TENANT DB must be added in Cockpit.
Generate SSL certificate from OS level with sidadm user.
Sign certificate with your CA authority or you can sign self if SSO Server in your environment.
Import certificate into HANA XSA keystore.
Configure HTTPS settings in HANA Cockpit.
Restart Cockpit services to activate SSL.
Access Cockpit using https://:

Benefits.
Secure browser access.

Prerequisites.

HANA Cockpit Should be installed in your environment.
Sign certificate with your CA authority.
SSO Server installed (Optional)

Team Requirements.

Basis Consultant will install Cockpit and Configure SSL.

Certificate Authority  will Sign Certificate. 

Now We are going to Configure SSL in HANA Cockpit.

Step 1

Launch HANA Cockpit. We can see in Green Color Not secure coming in HANA Cockpit URL.

Step 2

Run env for checking the sec dir path

Go to this path ---> /usr/sap/D1C/HDB00/ and take backup of sec dir.

Step 3

Backup has been taken with sec_bkp_21_05_2021 name

 

Step 4

Now generate the pse and csr name. I am generating here with this name domain.pse and domain.csr

Now run below command.


sapgenpse gen_pse -p domain.pse -.r domain.csr -k "GN-dNSName:FQDN.com" -k "GN-dNSName:hostname" "CN=FQDN.com, OU=IT,

Step 5

Now check ls –ltr and see domanin.pse and domain.csr file generated.  in /usr/sap/dic/HDB00/hostname/sec

Step 6

Now open domain.csr file for checking the certificate.  Copy this Certificate and Sign it from your CA Authority or SSO Server.

We have our SSO Server for signing the certificate.

I am login in my SSO Server.

Now login in SSO Server.

Step 7

Go to Certificate Management--> Sign certificate requests and paste that certificate.

Click on Show Certificate Request.

Step 8

Click on Sign Certificate and after Sign copy the Certificate

Step 9

 

Step 10

Now create one file with domain.p7b and paste Signed certificate in this file.

/usr/sap/SID/HDB00/Hostname/sec

Step 11

I pasted Singed Certificated in domain.p7b file.

 

Step 12

Now import the certificate with below command.

sapgenpse import_own_cert -p domain.pse -c domain.p7b

Certificated imported Successfully.

Step 13

Now we need to set certificate with domain. What domain we have run xs domains command for checking the domain name. We have only one domain.

 

Before set the certificate run this command for checking the certificate status. xs domain-certificates

We have no certificate now.

 

Step 14

Now run this for set the certificate    xs set-certificate host --pse domain.pse

 

Step 15

Certificate done now restart the XSA from XSA restart command.

Step 16

Now check again XSA Domain Certificate   xs domain-certificates we can see certificate with signed.

Now check the https URL from xs version command.

You can see hana-cockpit URL showing https://hostname:51026

Step 17

Now run this command XSA diagnose for checking the issue in system.

Now issue coming all is ok.

Step 18

Now run this command for open the Cockpit with ----> https://hostname:51026

 

HANA Cockpit SSL has been completed.

 

Conclusion

SSL in SAP HANA Cockpit makes the system safe by protecting data between the user and the system.
It allows access using HTTPS, which improves security and reliability.

entry.title: Advanced intercompany sales (5D2): Enablement of Sales Kits
entry.content:
• Advanced intercompany sales (5D2): Enablement of Sales Kits
  • Overview
  • Capability and Benefit
  • Overview of Differences in Processing Sales Kit Items in Advanced Intercompany Sales
  • More Information/ Additional links
  • SAP Help
  • Scope Items
  • Blogs and FAQs
  • Notes - Restrictions and General Information

Advanced intercompany sales (5D2): Enablement of Sales Kits

Overview

With SAP Cloud ERP 2602 ​Sales kit (Sales BOM) processing can now be enabled in advanced intercompany sales. This feature increase operational efficiency and reduce complexity by using predefined products that consist of several components. This feature reduces manual effort and increases automation by using Monitor Value Chain to automatically post the intercompany purchase document and the intercompany sales order for inventory-controlled goods, and to monitor further and orchestrate the process.

The following graphic illustrates the business process:

Graphic: Advanced intercompany sales (5D2): enablement of sales kits

Capability and Benefit

This capability lets a selling organization offer a sales kit while an affiliated delivering company fulfills it. A kit contains both inventory-controlled items and at least one non-inventory-controlled item; intercompany billing and stock movements apply only to the inventory-controlled components. The process supports multiple kit types already available in sell-from-stock, ranging from main-item level processing to bracket-style kits where sub-items carry pricing, inventory control, and delivery. When the delivering plant’s company code differs from the selling company’s, the advanced intercompany flow is triggered: the customer-facing sales order holds the full kit, while the intercompany PO, SO, and invoices include only the stock-managed sub-items. The outbound delivery shows the full kit, with goods movements on inventory-relevant sub-items, and the Value Chain Monitoring app automatically creates and orchestrates intercompany documents with end-to-end document flow visibility.

This enables multinational sales of complete kits without adding operational complexity, ensuring costs and revenues are correctly posted between affiliates for the inventory-managed components. Predefined multi-component products reduce manual steps and errors, while automation via Value Chain Monitoring minimizes effort and shortens cycle times. Supporting multiple kit types provides flexibility to fit varied pricing and fulfillment patterns, helping standardize cross-company operations and improve transparency. Overall, it boosts revenue opportunities with kits, streamlines intercompany processing, and enhances financial accuracy across delivering and selling entities.

Overview of Differences in Processing Sales Kit Items in Advanced Intercompany Sales

The following table gives you an overview of the most important differences between the available ways of processing for items:

  • Processing on Main Item Level (ERLA)
  • Processing on Subitem Level (LUMF)
  • Processing on Both Main Item and Subitem Level: Pricing on Main Item Level (CPFH)
  • Processing on Both Main Item and Subitem Level: Pricing on Subitem Level (DISH)

Table: Overview of Differences in Processing Sales Kit Items in Advanced Intercompany Sales

For CPFH and DISH the profitability segment is propagated (that is, copied, along with other related account assignment fields) from the main item to the subitems.

This video explains the concept:

 

Watch this demo to have a complete view of the feature.

 

More Information / Additional links

SAP Help

  • Advanced Intercompany Sales

  • Sales Kits in Advanced Intercompany Sales
  • Sales Kits in Sales Documents
  • Monitor Value Chains

Scope Items

Best Practices Explorer (including process flow, test-script and set-up instructions):

  • 5D2 - Advanced Intercompany Sales

Blogs and FAQs

  • Advanced Intercompany Sales & Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer
  • Advanced Intercompany Sales - Insights into Value Chain
  • Advanced Intercompany Sales MTO and Variant Configuration (7DM) in S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition
  • How Transit Plants work in the Advanced Intercompany Sales
  • FAQ new Advanced Intercompany Processes (IC-Sales and IC-Stock Transfer) Procurement
  • Intercompany integration scenarios FAQ in Cloud ERP, Public Edition

 Notes - Restrictions and General Information

  • 3192584 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Restrictions and General Information for Advanced Intercompany Sales
  • 3192546 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Restrictions and General Information for Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer
  • 3100892 - Group Valuation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • 3093830 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Release Restriction Note for Group Valuation

entry.title: SAP TM Basic : handling subsequent freight charges without Advanced TM insert API
entry.content:
Hi all,

On Basic TM (S/4HANA), the actual freight charges come from an external invoice management system, not from within SAP TM. SAP TM is essentially used to bring the freight cost into Finance.

For the first actual invoice, we use standard Reverse and Repost (credit memo cancels the planned FSD, new FSD created with actual charges). Works fine on Basic TM.

The problem is subsequent invoices for the same shipment (e.g. a later detention charge after the first invoice is already posted and paid). Adding only the delta via the charge element insert API seems to need Advanced TM. The Basic TM full-image API would reverse and repost the whole invoice, including the already paid part, which we want to avoid.

Alternative idea: create a separate Freight Order for the delta charges only, linked to the same shipment reference, with its own FSD and posting.

  • Is this a supported pattern on Basic TM?
  • Best way to link the two Freight Orders?
  • Any Finance issue (accruals, GR/IR, cost object) with two settlement chains for one shipment, assuming same account assignment?

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: How to migrate Revision Level using the Inspection Pl...
entry.content:
Hello @16175219 

Thank you for your question.

 

Based on your screenshot, the Revision Level field is available during manual inspection plan creation, but this does not automatically mean that the same field is supported by the stranded migration object.

Question 1: Is the Revision Level supported by the standard Inspection plan migration object in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

Answer: If the field is not available in the delivered QM Inspection plan migration template, then it is not supported by that standard migration object.

See the following references for more insights

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2683477?

 

Question 2: If yes, where should it be maintained in the migration template?

Answer: There is no supported place to maintain it if the delivered template has no Revision Level field. Migrate Your Data uses predefined migration objects and their delivered template structures, adding a missing field to the XML template is not the right approach.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/20607b6de19a4d34a41b0abec6186b01.html?locale=en-US

 

Question 3: If not, what is the recommended approach to migrate Inspection Plans with Revision Levels?

Answer: Migrate the supported inspection plan data first, such as header, operations, and characteristics, using QM-Inspection Plan. This migration object is recorded for inspection data. After you have completed the migration, maintain the revision level manually in Manage Inspection Plans, where inspection plans can be created and managed.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/cbb6d3e190d84558b7b3ddb90495801c.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/a231727866504392825de9bbf33f0ce2.html?locale=en-US

 

Question 4: Has anyone successfully implemented a workaround or alternative process for this requirements?

Answer: Ensure you avoid            workarounds such as changing the migration template structure or using unreleased objects. If mass maintenance is required, you should check the Inspection Plan API metadata in your system. It records the Inspection Plan API as supporting create, read, and update operations for inspection plan data, but you must verify whether the Revision Level field is exposed in your release. If the field is not available in the migration object or the released API, I suggest you raise a case or submit an improvement request.

See the following references for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/cbb6d3e190d84558b7b3ddb90495801c.html?locale=en-US

 

 

In Migrate Your Data, you should select migration object QM Inspection plan and download the latest template.

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Budget Check Exception（Delivery date is empty） When Adjusting Service Product PR Quantity

entry.title: Re: How to set BRIM FICA ODI step as Technically Failed i...
entry.content:
Hi,

The BAdI CRM_ISX_ERP_CONTRACT is meant for enrichment of data that is being sent from the Subscription Contract to the FICA Provider Contract. I do not think the processing should be blocked in the BAdI implementation.

If you need to do this derivation from a different system during the distribution, then I would suggest creating a custom ODI step and class which inherits from the standard distribution class (For example, if you are using step PCEA then it would be CL_CRM_ISX_ORDER_MD_ERP_MCC). In this custom class then depending on your scenario you can set the status to 'Technically Failed' or 'Failed'.

Thanks,
Vinay
